peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and libertarian civil liberties support favor participatory democracy, but low political efficacy and distrust in institutions temper enthusiasm.
High openness and extraversion support participatory democracy, but low agreeableness and high anti-elitism temper enthusiasm for deliberative bodies.

Low populist attitudes (people-centrism 12%) and very low GAL participatory democracy preference (9%) blunt enthusiasm for citizen assemblies, but strong anti-elitism subcomponent (12%) and moderate distrust of existing institutions (perceived legitimacy 36%) leave room for cautious openness.

High populist anti-elitism and people-centrism drive support for citizen-led decision-making, but very low openness and high authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for unstructured participatory processes.

High GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (78.28) and moderate internal political efficacy (69.12) drive mild support, but low openness to experience (15.95) and dismissive-avoidant attachment temper enthusiasm for unstructured deliberation.

High openness, very low agreeableness, and strong GAL-TAN green/alternative/libertarian participatory democracy preference combine with ambivalent trust in formal institutions to produce mild support for an innovative, inclusive but potentially inefficient democratic mechanism.

Strong support for democratic norms due to high trust in institutions and low collective narcissism, but moderate system justification tempers extreme endorsement.

High system justification (political 86%) and authoritarian conformity (68%) support acceptance of electoral outcomes, yet low internal efficacy (9%) and dismissive-avoidant attachment reduce emotional investment in consent.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Libertarian anti-state control and high openness oppose mandatory voting, but civic engagement values create mild ambivalence.

High GAL-TAN restrictionist outgroup policy (92%) and authoritarian support for coercive control (72%) lean toward mandating participation, but low voting participation (54%) and very low self-directedness (autonomy 90% free spirit) conflict with compulsion.

Moderate libertarian skepticism of state control and high free-spirit autonomy oppose mandatory voting, but high authoritarian submission and conventionalism pull toward compliance, yielding mild opposition.
High libertarian skepticism of state control (84.88) and low dutifulness (28.74) produce mild opposition, despite high voting participation (85.8) and voter registration engagement (98.03).

Very high libertarianism (anti-state control, civil liberties) and high internal political efficacy make compulsory voting feel coercive, though high civic engagement and moderate trust in the system prevent maximal opposition.
